speaker: interviewee
text: [TEST PATTERN TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Date, April 2nd, 1995. Survivor, Eva Kor. Last name at birth, Mozes. Interviewer, Doris Lazarus. City and state, Chicago, Illinois. Country, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Doris Lazarus. This is Eva Moses Kor. I am here to conduct her interview. Today's date is April 2nd, 1995. And we are in Chicago, Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Doris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eva Mozes Kor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-V-A M-O-Z-E-S K-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Mozes, E-V-A M-O-Z-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 31, 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: According to that, I am 61 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of your hometown? And spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Portz. It's a village-- P-O-R-T-Z-- in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: My immediate family members-- my father, Alexander Mozes. My mother, Jaffa, J-A-F-F-A Mozes. Her maiden name was Herch, H-E-R-C-H, I think. And Edit Mozes-- E-D-I-T. Elis, or Elis in Hungarian-- E-L-I-Z. And my twin sister Miriam-- M-I-R-I-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, two grandparents. And I do not remember their first names, because they used to call them Zidy and Bobby. These were my mother's parents, and their name was Hersch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names that you just mentioned of your family, where those sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edit, Elise, and Miriam were all my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember much about your home and your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a village. I'm sorry. Yes. It was a very small village-- about a hundred families, one dusty road leading from one end to the other. And this was basically a farming village-- very, very primitive-- no running water, no electricity, no paved roads. These were all farmers. And my father was a farmer as well, and a landowner. We owned quite a lot of land surrounding the village. And our home was a big farm house with big, white columns with an open corridor. It had a lot of other buildings, farm buildings. One was for the horses-- a barn for the horses and for the cows and for the hay and for the wheat to be stored, the corn to be stored. And we had chickens and then geese and ducks and sheep, and a winery. It was a very, very big active, and wealthy farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely religious-- very observant. On Saturday, we could not carry a handkerchief unless we tied it to our hand. My mother was all wearing some kind of a scarf or turban, or she was wearing a wig. My father prayed every morning, and always before meals and dinner in the evening. And we always said [NON-ENGLISH] when we went to bed. With Saturday, unfortunately, I didn't like it. We used to have to finish the whole Saturday prayer book. And that was because my father very much wanted a boy. And see, he didn't have any boys, so the girls had to do it. And all I really understood from all these words is [NON-ENGLISH], because it kept coming up so often. But I knew how to read Hebrew. And the rest of the words, I didn't understand, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian. My parents spoke Yiddish. And unfortunately, they did not want us to learn. Because see, by the time we were born, our destiny was sealed. And my parents knew that as we were growing up, that things were very bad. And they wanted to be able to speak between the two of them so we the children wouldn't understand it. And that's what they did. They never spoke to us in Yiddish, but I would hear them speak in the other room Yiddish. And so we spoke just Hungarian. We did have-- in 19-- I think-- 42 we had a fraulein, Jewish, who came to our village, to our house, to teach my two older sisters. Because they finished already grade school. And my mother did not want-- my mother did not want that her daughter would not be educated. She was very determined that her girls would be educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father said, what's the big deal? They are going to get married anyway, have children. Why do they need an education? My mother said no way. I'm not giving up. So the compromise was that they would bring a fraulein who would work with my two older sisters, prepare them for tests, and then they could go to the city. The other reason that my father was afraid that they would live in the city, that they would become not religious. And that was a very big worry for him. So we had a fraulein who would prepare them. And then Mom not to make us feel like we were left out, she would teach us a little bit of German so we would be included. Do you remember much about the school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a one-room schoolhouse. And actually, the first year-- in 1940, in the fall of 1940-- we could not be registered. And this is why I have a little bit of a controversy about my age, because I don't have a birth certificate. They would not register anybody who wasn't 6. Well, if I was born in '34, I should have been six years old. And so my mother said they will be six in January. And they would-- they put us in the classroom, and they said we'll try to see how it works. Well, in January, they registered us. So I am not really sure what the story is. But it was a one-room schoolhouse with all the village kids. Because I never attended before occupation. The occupation occurred in 1940. Among the first things that I have learned, actually, in first grade-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- probably it was in the spring of 1941-- that the law of the land was designed in such a way that Jews could be harassed, punished, and even killed without any kind of retribution or justice. The people who were perpetrating it were encouraged to do so. In my first experience, which really is very, very vivid in my mind, we were very good students. We learned very fast, and we loved to go to school, because it was a magical place that you learned so many things. And one day, the teacher came dressed in a very nice spring dress and sat down on the chair. And you could hear that something was crackling there. Well, the boys in the village liked to play tricks. They put some bird eggs on her chair. There was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: She became very upset and started yelling and asked who did it. So all of a sudden, all the kids said the two dirty Jews did it. And there was-- what bothered me the most, that the teacher never investigated anything. Why would two little girls go climb in trees and get bird eggs? We were-- and the children were all against us. And they knew that they could get away with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were ordered to kneel on corn kernels for one hour. And all our objections and all our demands to have an investigation fell on deaf ears. And they all just kept looking at us and calling us dirty Jews. The unfairness of that moment is really very strongly engraved in my memory. Because I think that children have a very strong sense of fairness. We went home, and I was crying. And my mother was in the front yard weeding her flower garden. And she took us in her arms, and she cried with us. And she said, well, [NON-ENGLISH]. There isn't anything that I can do for you. Somehow, you're very young, but you have to learn that you have to take it. And I was very disappointed in my mother. I expected, as I think most children expect, for their mother to protect them. And I expected my mother to march to school and talk to the teacher. But she didn't. [SOB]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- somehow, I had to get over it. I don't think that I have ever accepted the fact-- not only that. They kept calling us dirty Jews. I knew that I was cleaner than most of the other kids. The books in school were filled with hatred against Jews. One of the math problems that I clearly remember, it said, when you have five Jews, and you kill three, how many are left? And the reason that it's important to illustrate-- for people to understand that everything surrounding the hatred and destruction of the Jews was part of everyday life. It wasn't just one little place. There was no place for us to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I think that living in this-- we still went to school. We tried to stay out of trouble. But we got spit on. We got pushed around. And of course, we couldn't go home to mother and complain, or to father, because that didn't help, either. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By 1943, things got pretty tough. Our house was often surrounded by Nazi young hoodlums who were harassing us. So we attended school-- not always-- could-- could not always could even leave the house. We had to wear a yellow Star of David. And things got even more intense at home and in school when we did go. I cannot-- I think that most people cannot understand a school that would teach, endorse, and support hatred against one group or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents' fear? Did they ever talk about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, not fast enough. [SNIFFLING] What happened-- and I try to analyze it. My father was very religious, prayed to God. And he thought God was going to make some kind of a miracle. I think that was one of the problem for the Jewish people in Europe. The other one-- I think nobody ever believes that the worst thing will happen to them. So I heard my parents say, well, we live in a small village. Yeah, my father had to go every two weeks to the nearby village to present himself there, like almost like being on parole. If not, they would come to arrest him. But they felt that they were so far away that we would-- we would not be deported. That somehow, that was as bad as it was going to get. They always felt that-- maybe I heard people talk about being in the city, that in the city, there were so many people, so they wouldn't find them. Obviously, they found the people in the cities, and they found my parents and us in the village. And they had no problem finding us, because we were the only Jewish family. And nobody was willing to protect us. In 1943-- late in 1943-- my parents woke us up. So we were only one hour from the Romanian border. Woke us up in the middle of the night. It's quiet. Because we were-- by that time, we knew that Nazi youths were often surrounding the house. It looked like nobody was around. And so we started towards the end of our property going through the orchard. At the end of the orchard-- apple orchard-- there was the railway traffic. And we thought we could walk on the railroad track and reach the Romanian border. Mother was up front, the four girls between them, and my father was at the end. When we reached the gate of the apple orchard, we were surrounded by the Nazis. We turned back. And that was the only effort they ever tried. By-- by '43, it was too late. If they would have left maybe in '41-- I was very, very angry with my parents for a very long time. I do realize that they did the best they could. And nobody can do better than their best. I wish that they would have seen the tragedy coming the way I saw it coming. Because I could tell, [? scent ?], day in, day out, things were getting more difficult. One of the interesting things from how we learned in that small village what was going on-- in 1941, my father bought a radio-- battery-operated radio. And what they would do-- they would close the door when it was news time, and they would listen to the news. Well, as I said, I was always very curious. And I put my ears to the door, because I wanted to know whar was-- what they were listening to that was so important. And I could hear reports from Hitler. And he was yelling on the radio that he was going to rule the world, and he was going to kill all the Jews. And so I asked my parents about it. And they said, well, don't worry. They are not going to come here. That is far away. That's happening in Germany. You don't have anything to worry about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even when the thing happened in school-- the situation with the-- with the teacher and the punishment, even then, when I asked my mother, I said, well, did Hitler come here? You said he's far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, well, it's not what really happened. But the Hungarian army joined the Nazis. They became their allies. But they are not going to come here for us. I think that grownups obviously have a little bit more difficulty understanding and being in touch with what's really going on. Because I could clearly see that things were getting bad. One very painful experience in 1943-- actually, there are two. We were told to go to school to watch a-- something on the wall. We [INAUDIBLE] called it jumping pictures on the wall. We didn't know it was called a movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were projecting it on the schoolhouse [INAUDIBLE] at night. And it has to be dark. This was the first movie that I have ever seen. And you know what the movie was all about? How to catch and kill a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went home, and I told my-- my mother about it. And of course, she didn't know. Again, there was nothing she could do. When our house was surrounded by Nazi youths-- these were-- some of the kids from the village, but they were very good at joining in harassment. Some of them were from maybe nearby villages, Hungarians. And they would just throw tomatoes, rocks, eggs, and insults. But it wouldn't stop for hours and hours. You could not leave the house. And I asked my father-- I said, Daddy, please, go and stop them. I can't stand it anymore. I would have gone outside by myself. I was not really afraid. But I would have never, ever dared disobey their orders. Never. I was a very good, obedient child. And my father said to me, you are so stupid. You are so stubborn. You never seem to understand it. It doesn't get into your head that there is nothing we can do about it. We just have to learn to take it. But I felt like I was cooped in an elevator that was burning, and there was no way out. And it was-- I was trapped inside, and there was nothing I could do about it. And this is what happened to me in 1967 in Terre Haute when the house was surrounded by-- by young kids who were playing all kinds of tricks on me. And again, emotionally, there was nothing I could do about it. If I went out to chase them, they came back with reinforcement. And emotionally, it threw me back to 1943. And so in 1944-- winter of '43 and '44, my mother became very ill. In the meantime, there were all kinds of other laws were coming. And by beginning of '43 [SOBS] we had to let-- we had two maids who were helping my mother. And we had three families who worked with my father on the farm. We had a quarter for the servants who were working on the farm. And Jews could not hire anybody who was not Jewish. So we let-- we had to let them go. And my father tried to work as much of the farm as he could. And we helped. We help with around the farm. We knew how to raise vegetables and water, get water from the well, pull it up, and water the garden. And we tried to go around the best-- I mean we were pretty happy most of the time until we had to deal with the news or with any kind of personal harassment. Then by-- And the other thing was that my mother wanted to go and visit my grandmother and my grandfather and her brother. And you could not travel without special permit. And I think in the four years of occupation, maybe she got twice a permit to go, and we went to see our grandparents. And then, towards the end, of course, permit was never granted. Even I know on one occasion, my grandmother was quite ill, and my mother felt so badly that she could not go. And she was the only daughter to be with my grandmother. Things were getting, as I said, from bad to worse, and we could sense it. There was no doubt about it, that things are going to be-- that it never seemed to improve. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only-- and my mother became very ill in '43. Late winter of '43, she developed typhoid. She was between life and death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, we needed somebody Jewish. We couldn't have anybody from the village helping, because that was against the law. So my father went to the city and hired a nurse to come and help take care of my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was so fragile that she couldn't even turn from side to side. And she barely survived. She was very weak, still, in the-- in February, in March of '44 when we were ordered one day-- four Hungarian gendarmes came and said, pack food for two weeks. We are taking you to a ghetto, so then could resettle you in Hungary. And all the villagers came-- even my best friend Luchi, who was a minister's daughter. Nobody-- not one of them-- said I am sorry. They didn't-- they probably couldn't do very much, but at least I would have liked to hear them say we are sorry for what's going on. Nobody. I think probably many of the villagers were quite eager to get into our home and ransack it, as they obviously did. We packed food, whatever we had. My mother's bread was baked. [SNIFFLES] And we took food. We were taken to the ghetto of Simleu Silvaniei.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, the dates, I do not know. I know this time only that it was just after Passover. So if Passover came early or late, I do not know. I really do not know. But [BLOWING NOSE] in the ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a very unusual ghetto. It had no buildings. Only one [SNIFFLES] structure. There was a river, the [INAUDIBLE] that went between-- the-- in the middle of the camp. And the commandant-- I am convinced that all ghetto commandants had earned their right to be a commandant by proving their cruelty. This one definitely proved his.

speaker: interviewer
text: We built little tents out of our sheet. And this is where we were living. Well, he had a great joy in-- as soon as it got a little bit cloudy, they would tear your tents down and put them on the other side of the river. And then he would stand there and laugh and say, oh, isn't that interesting to see the children of Israel living in tents and getting soaked to the bone? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We managed somehow. We ran out of food. One day, finally, they took my father for interrogation. And the rumor was, of course, we knew that everybody was being taken into that big building and coming out on stretchers being beaten up. Well, they brought my father back. And you could smell. All his fingernails and toenails were burned with candles. They whipped him with a whip on his back. He had marks all over his back. And they said-- they kept telling him they didn't believe that he didn't have any buried gold and silver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess they-- we stayed in the ghetto more than two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the name of the ghetto? Simleu Silvaniei, Szilagysomnyo in Hungarian. It would be S-Z-I-L-A-G-Y, because that's a Hungarian G. Szilag-- somnyo-- S-O-M-N-Y-O, Hungarian spelling. And the-- the reason was that they kept us five weeks, because they had to interrogate everybody. They knew, that obviously, that we were being taken to Auschwitz. And they wanted to put their-- get their hands on whatever money they could get from the Jews. OK. If we stop right here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.
